pullRequest.title: For MacOS app, remove redundant adhoc sign during ``briefcase package`` or ``briefcase package -u``
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
Detect when a `build` command is being executed as part of a `package` command and skip the redundant adhoc signing of the app during the build. This is safe to do because the adhoc signing will happen later in the package command itself.

<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
When running `briefcase package -u` on MacOS the app was being adhoc signed twice - once during the build and once during the package. The adhoc signing during the build is redundant since the adhoc signing will happen during the packaging.

<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
Fixes #1099

pullRequest.title: Verify system requirements when running `dev` on Linux
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #1137.

During live tutorials, a common failure mode for Linux users is to *not* have the required system dependencies installed; this then manifests as obscure errors during the `briefcase create` process when pygobject can't be compiled because of a pycairo dependency. However, unless you know what you're looking at, it's an extremely impenetrable error message.

Historically, this would have been difficult to resolve because `dev` wasn't platform aware; however, as of #2419, it is. This means we're now in a position to use the platform-specific tooling of the `system` backend to check system dependencies.

This PR modifies the `briefcase dev linux system` command (which is the default `briefcase dev` on Linux) to invoke the system package checks prior to installing development dependencies. Since a fresh Briefcase Hello World that specifies a Toga backend will have all the platform dependencies defined, this means you'll get a notification that you need to `apt install` (or whatever) a specific set of packages if they're not available.

It also reorganizes the Linux system tooling; the  "Passive" and "Mostly Passive" commands might have meant something historically, but that distinction no longer exists - there are no uses of "Passive". This means we can have a base "System" Mixin that only operates locally, and is really only used by dev, but has all the tools to verify local system behavior; and a "Docker" mixin that extends "System" adding Docker-specific checks and flags.

pullRequest.title: Update to WiX 5
pullRequest.body:
* Closes #1185 

Corresponding template PRs:
* https://github.com/beeware/briefcase-windows-app-template/pull/14
  * This one contains the commit history and discussion.
* https://github.com/beeware/briefcase-windows-VisualStudio-template/pull/60

Main changes:
* WiX is now always used as a Briefcase-managed tool, and is automatically upgraded.
* The WiX build process has been greatly simplified, from 3 commands down to 1.
* The functionality of heat.exe has been replaced by a [`<Files>` element](https://docs.firegiant.com/wix/schema/wxs/files/) in the template. The "external app" mode therefore now only affects `briefcase create`, not `briefcase package`.

pullRequest.title: Use isolated development environments for dev mode
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #1735 

Modifies the `dev` command to use an isolated virtual environment by default. This means an app in dev mode runs in a "clean" virtual environment, only containing the dependencies needed to run the app. 

The old behavior of using the current venv can be restored by using the `--no-isolation` flag.

This PR also changes the name of the dev mode environment to include a platform identifier; this is a convenience so that it is possible for a project checkout to be shared across multiple Python versions and OS versions. This is mostly a convenience for the BeeWare team - I have a testbed app that I need to test on multiple OS versions and Python versions, so it helps to have a separate venv for each one, rather than re-creating every time you change OS or Python version. Most users will only ever have a single isolated development venv per project.

This will require a change to the BeeWare tutorial to adjust to the fact that `pip install` will no longer change the runtime environment for dev mode. This is also enough of a change to expectations about Briefcase that it warrants our next release being 0.4.0.

pullRequest.title: Add a check for iCloud after rolling out the template.
pullRequest.body:
iCloud creates (and aggressively maintains) resource forks on `.app` folders created on iCloud-synchronised drives. These resource forks cannot be removed (or rather, they can - but iCloud will put them back seconds later), but they also prevent app signing from succeeding.

This PR does a check for the resource fork after generating the app template; and again before Build and Package commands (the two commands that trigger signing); if iCloud markers are detected, an error is raised.

The check is performed during `briefcase create` immediately after generating the template; if a problem is detected, the macOS bundle is deleted (since the metadata seems to persist if the `.app` folder is copied to a non-synchronised location. Deletion is not performed on the build/package versions due to the potential for destroying user data, but the error message warns that deletion may be necessary.

As a final safety, the internal error code returned during signing is also surfaced.

Fixes #1808.

pullRequest.title: Android sdk img verify
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
Adds a ``list_installed_system_images()`` utility method to ``AndroidSDK`` that invokes ``sdkmanager --list_installed`` and returns a set of installed system image package identifiers. Updates ``verify_system_image()`` to use this method instead of only checking if the system image directory exists.
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
Previously Briefcase verified a system image by checking if the expected directory existed. This check is not strong enough as the directory may exist wile the system image is incomplete or corrupted. This would cause a confusing error when either ``avdmanager`` or the emulator would try to use an incomplete system image.
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
Fixes #1895

pullRequest.title: Allow use of Setuptools launchers
pullRequest.body:
Currently the application startup script is hard coded in the per-platform template.  
This is quite restrictive in that it only works if you have a module named 'app' with a function 'main' which returns an object with a function `main_loop` (on windows at least, other platforms can be different).

Also, on windows this means that in task manager you see `pythonw.exe` rather than the name of the distributed application.

To make briefcase more flexible I propose using the built in mechanism for defining entry points / applications. 
On windows this already creates exe launchers that have the desired application names. 
This also allows having multiple gui and console applications in the one installer package.

In this PR the windows launcher creation has been modified slightly to create relocatable exe applications in the contents/app folder.
Start menu shortcuts will be created for each of these rather than the static launcher currently defined in the template.
These executables will show  in task manager with the user specified names.

On linux, executable launch scripts will be created for each entrypoint defined.

On MacOS, if an entrypoint is defined with the same name as the `formal_name` specified it will be the main application. There are launch scripts for any others also defined in the bundle/Contents/MacOS the an end user could run from the command line etc. 

Mobile platforms have not been adapted to this mechanism as there's no way to produce multiple executables. 
If desired however they could also be updated in the same way to allow defining the custom entry point which would be used in preference to the templated method.

If no entry_points are defined it falls back to the launching mechanism as defined in the platform templates.

pullRequest.title: Use PEP639 license format internally.
pullRequest.body:
Switch to using PEP 639 license metadata internally. 

* Existing projects without a license declaration get a warning, a dummy licence file, and an SPDX license declaration of `LicenseRef-UnknownLicense`
* Existing projects in pre-PEP621 `license` format have the value of `license` written to a dummy file, and a SPDX-compliant value of `license` derived from the contents of the existing `license` field. A value of `LicenseRef-UnknownLicense` is used if a license cannot be determined.
* Existing projects in PEP621 `license.text` format are treated the same as pre-PEP621 `license` format
* Existing projects in PEP621 `license.file` format have the location turned into `license-files`, and a `license` value guessed from the license text. A value of `LicenseRef-UnknownLicense` is used if a license cannot be determined.
* Any value provided in `license-files` (before or after coercion to PEP 639) must exist.

If a dummy license file is needed, it is written into the `build` directory as `build/license_file.<app name>.txt`

As a result of the config parsing changes, we can now assume that full license files are *always* available when building an app. This significantly simplifies the app builds.

Disclosure: This PR was generated with the use of SpecKit and Claude Sonnet 4.6, but has had substantial manual modifications.

Fixes #2146.
Refs beeware/briefcase-template#249 which makes the PEP639 change to the default app template.

pullRequest.title: Improving build error messages when build fails due to missing wheels
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
# Description of changes #
I added more helpful error messages (via the `install_hint` parameter on `_pip_install`) when `build iOS` and `build macOS` fail. I also added a new `install_hint` parameter to `_install_app_requirements` per suggestion so that it could be passed in directly instead of having to get passed in via `pip_kwargs`.



<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
# What problem is solved #
`build macOS` and `build iOS` commands can sometimes fail because of a single missing wheel for a particular platform's version of a library.

Ex. 1 - a project that relies on a version of `numpy` before 1.2.0 won't be able to build on macOS if `universal_build=true` because there's no arm64 wheel for numpy versions before 1.2.0.

Ex. 2 - accidentally deleting the `iphonesimulator` wheel will cause `build iOS` to fail even if `iphoneos` is present but the error message won't indicate this / will make it sound like both wheels are missing.

<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
# What issue is this PR related to #
Fixes #2230

pullRequest.title: Use git config values as default author and email
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
This PR improves the default values for "Author" and "Author's Email" in `briefcase new` and `briefcase convert` by reading the user's git configuration.

Fixes #2269 

## Why?
Currently, the default values are:
* `Author`: Jane Developer
* `Author's Email`: firstname@domain (e.g. `niklas@example.org` from Author "Niklas Mertsch" and domain "org.example")

This PR checks for the git config values `user.name` and `user.email`. If they are set, they are used instead of the current default values.

## Details
#### New method `BaseCommand.get_git_config_value(section: str, option: str) -> str | None`
* Read and merge from the following files, if present:
    * system config (`/etc/git/config`)
    * global config (`~/.gitconfig`)
    * user config (`~/.config/git/config`)
    * repo config (`./.git/config`)

#### Updated default values in `NewCommand` and `ConvertCommand`
* If `self.get_git_config_value("user", "name"/"email")` is not `None`:
    * Use it as default value.
    * Add sentence "Based on your git configuration, we believe it could be '{value}'." to the prompt intro (wording copied from `ConvertCommand` email handling).
* Otherwise, use the current default value (e.g. "Jane Developer").

## Comments for reviewers
* I chose to only use the git username if no "PEP 621 'authors'" were found.
* I don't know if `BaseCommand` really is the right place for `get_git_config_value()`.  I chose it because it is the base class of `NewCommand` and `ConvertCommand`, and because it provides `self.tools.git` so we don't have to guard the `git` import in multiple places.
* I chose not to test that `get_git_config_value()` does what it is doing. The implementation is trivial, and I can't find a way to test it without actually just testing mocks.
* I chose to test the new author and email functionality for `NewCommand` together, not in one test for author and one for email. That's because both are set in the same method (`NewCommand.build_app_context()`), so splitting the tests seemed to add too much code duplication. Splitting `build_app_context()` into smaller methods might be the right approach here, but I didn't want to take that decision for you.

## Open questions for reviewers
* The UX improvement is nice to have, but I'm not satisfied with the complexity my PR adds to the repo. What do you think?
* I don't understand if/where the default values from [briefcase-template](briefcase-template) are used. Can I ignore that repo for this change?
* To keep `command.tools.git` out of the general test setup, I check for its presence in `get_git_config_value()`. This check should always be true, so I added `self.console.warning(...)` in that case. Is that the right way to handle this?

## PR Checklist:
- [x] Implement feature
    - [x] Implement `git config` as optional source for default values
    - [x] Tell users where the default values are coming from
- [x] All new features have been tested
    - [x] `get_git_config_value()` reads correct config files
    - [x] `NewCommand` uses git values if present
    - [x] `ConvertCommand` uses git values if present
- [x] All new features have been documented
- [x] I have read the **CONTRIBUTING.md** file
- [x] I will abide by the code of conduct

pullRequest.title: Update ditto unit test to allow for recent changes to MacOS zip files
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
In `test_ditto()`, filter out any files that start with `__MACOS/` from `archive.namelist()` results before comparing to the expected output.
 
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
`test_ditto()` is failing on the main branch on newer versions of MacOS (tested on 15.4.1) because zip archives (now) contain Mac filesystem resource information in the archive. These resources are all prefixed with `__MACOS/`. However these are not "real" files, but they do show up in a file listing of the zip archive. Since this does not match the expected results, the test is failing.

<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
Fixes #2295

pullRequest.title: Use the system certificate store for downloads, where possible.
pullRequest.body:
Introduces the use of `truststore` when performing downloads in Briefcase. This should hopefully avoid the issues with the use of proxies and VPNs. httpx uses certifi as a trust store by default; but corporate proxy/VPN configurations often involve manipulation of the system trust store. This leads to situations where a URL can be reached by a browser, but *not* by Briefcase. Using `truststore` means that Briefcase will have the same trust configuration as the browser.

Fixes #2296.

pullRequest.title: Enable Briefcase to package apps that weren't build with Briefcase
pullRequest.body:
Briefcase is not the only tool for converting Python applications into running binaries. However, most of those tools don't provide support for signing and notarization, or packaging as DMG/PKG (on macOS), or MSI (on Windows), or DEB/RPM (on Linux). 

This PR modifies Briefcase to add the concept of an "external app" - an app that wasn't built with Briefcase, but *is* being packaged by Briefcase. This is done by adding a `package_path` configuration item. 

Windows requires a template change to reflect possible deep binary paths:
* beeware/briefcase-windows-app-template#66
* beeware/briefcase-windows-VisualStudio-template#59

Fixes #2326.

pullRequest.title: Resolve CXXX Ruff  errors (previously ignored)
pullRequest.body:
Resolves all of the places where Ruff complains about comprehension errors (C400, C408, C414, C416). The vast majority were C408 - Ruff requires using a dictionary literal instead of a call to `dict()` wherever possible. These were mostly in tests.

With those errors no longer present (from the transition from Flake8 to Ruff), those errors are no longer ignored in `pyproject.toml`

Fixes #2382

pullRequest.title: Resolve SIMXXX
pullRequest.body:
Handle [flake8-simplify ruff rules](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/#flake8-simplify-sim).

This PR build on the stale PR #2475 by @cgkoutzigiannis. I added the following:
* Rebase to `main`
* 670feba6: Fix failing test (close file before moving it)
* e29472d8, e29472d8: Use strings instead of lists for cmdline test inputs ([requested](https://github.com/beeware/briefcase/pull/2475#discussion_r2346407120) by @mhsmith))
* a3abfff8: Activate `SIM` rules, ignore [SIM108](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/if-else-block-instead-of-if-exp/) (enforce ternary operator, too opinionated)
* Handle remaining SIM rules:
    * b7f96a13: Refactor nested `if`'s ([SIM102](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/collapsible-if/))
    * 48cfe755: Refactor `return True/False` pattern ([SIM103](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/needless-bool/))
    * 081c7552: Use `contextlib.suppress` ([SIM105](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/suppressible-exception/), reverted in 59b8b53614c9d8a1bf46c5dc899dcf0120a11ee9)
    * edbb8639: Use `open` with context manager ([SIM115](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/open-file-with-context-handler/))

Fixes #2384.
Closes #2475.

pullRequest.title: Check the system Python version, not the filename
pullRequest.body:
Briefcase checks if it being run with the same Python version as the system Python version.

On Linux, this check is implemented by checking the real filename of the symlink `/usr/bin/python3` (e.g. `python3.12`). This does not work on Gentoo Linux, as reported in #2490.

With this PR, briefcase instead executes `/usr/bin/python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.version_info[:2])"` to obtain the system Python version.

Fixes #2490

pullRequest.title: briefcase dev: Don't overwrite existing environment variables
pullRequest.body:
`briefcase dev`: Only set `PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1`, `PYTHONDEVMODE=1`, and `PYTHONUTF8=1` if these variables are not already set.

#2497 is only about `PYTHONDEVMODE`, but I think this generic implementation is less surprising to users. I might lack context, though.

(I fixed two very minor typos along the way, but don't think this is worth a towncrier fragment.)

Fixes #2497

pullRequest.title: Add support for uninstall options
pullRequest.body:
A companion for #2496 - adds user-configurable pre-uninstall options as part of the uninstall process.

The GUI for these options will only be visible if the user re-runs the MSI installer. This appears to be a "feature" of the Windows "remove software" tooling - even if you modify the uninstaller registry entry to call `msiexec /qf /I...`, the GUI is not displayed. The default value for options will be honoured, though.

As with #2496 - most of the heavy lifting is actually done by the templates, not this PR:
* beeware/briefcase-windows-app-template#79
* beeware/briefcase-windows-visualstudio-template#74

Fixes #2573.

pullRequest.title: Add -a/--app support to briefcase open command
pullRequest.body:
## What does this change do?

Adds support for the `-a / --app` option to the `briefcase open` command, allowing a single app to be opened when multiple apps exist in a project.

## Why is this needed?

Currently, `briefcase open` opens all apps in a project and does not support selecting a specific app via command-line arguments. This brings `open` in line with other commands such as `update` that already support `-a / --app`.

## What was changed?

- Added `-a / --app` option handling to the `open` command
- Ensured only the selected app is finalized and opened
- Added tests analogous to `test_update_app_single` under `tests/commands/open`

Fixes #2635

pullRequest.title: Bump PySide6 to ~=6.10, macOS min to 13.0, add Python version to error messages
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #2658

`PySide6~=6.8` doesn't ship wheels for Python 3.14, so builds fail with a confusing
"no compatible wheel" error that doesn't mention the Python version. The maintainer
outlined the fix in the issue thread.

Changes:
- Bump `PySide6-Essentials` and `PySide6-Addons` from `~=6.8` to `~=6.10` in both
  `src/briefcase/bootstraps/pyside6.py` and `automation/src/automation/bootstraps/pyside6.py`
- Bump macOS `min_os_version` from `12.0` to `13.0` (PySide6 6.10 requires it)
- Include the Python version tag in the wheel-not-found error messages on macOS and iOS,
  so users can tell whether it's a Python version issue or an architecture issue
- Updated tests and added a changenote

pullRequest.title: Document minimal configuration for packaging external apps
pullRequest.body:
## Description

This PR adds a minimal `pyproject.toml` configuration example to the
**"Packaging external apps"** HOWTO.

The current documentation explains how to configure an external app
using `external_package_path`, but it does not include a minimal working
configuration example. This update provides a concise example to help
users understand the required structure when packaging an existing project.

It also clarifies that `briefcase package` should be run from the
directory containing the `pyproject.toml` file, making the workflow
more explicit for new users.

Fixes #2668

pullRequest.title: Ensure multi-tagged wheels are correctly identified as universal
pullRequest.body:
If a macOS binary wheel has multiple platform tags, Briefcase's platform merging code won't correctly identify this fact, and will mark the wheel as requiring merging. This is because the WHEEL metadata is a multi-valued header - it contains an independent `Tag` entry for each tag in the wheel.

The example package that revealed this was `orjson`, which is tagged `cp313-cp313-macosx_10_15_x86_64.macosx_11_0_arm64.macosx_10_15_universal2.whl`. The wheel metadata for this wheel reads:

```
Wheel-Version: 1.0
Generator: maturin (1.11.5)
Root-Is-Purelib: false
Tag: cp313-cp313-macosx_10_15_x86_64
Tag: cp313-cp313-macosx_11_0_arm64
Tag: cp313-cp313-macosx_10_15_universal2
```

Briefcase identifies the tag for this wheel as `arm64`, because this is the value for the `Tag` header that is returned from the WHEEL file (presumably because it is alphabetically last). This means Briefcase doesn't identify the wheel as universal, and so it installs a second copy of the same wheel as the "other" architecture, and then fails on merging because there is an existing x86-64 slice in the binaries being merged.

This PR adds multi-tag handling to WHEEL file processing, ensuring that *any* universal2 entry is identified as a universal wheel. It also ensures that if a wheel is multi-tagged as arm64 and x86-64, that is also identified as effectively universal (as no merging is required).

Fixes #2690.

pullRequest.title: Use packaging for version parsing
pullRequest.body:
The regex defined in PEP 440 is no longer an accurate definition for all valid version schemes; `packaging.version` provides the canonical version number parsing mechanism.

This PR replaces the custom PEP 440 validator with the `packaging.version.Version` parser. This means we now accept a wider range of version numbers as valid inputs (including `v1.2.3`, `1.2RC1` and `25.09.2`), but internally provides a clean, canonical, parsed version for use by the rest of Briefcase.

Fixes #2693.

pullRequest.title: Ensure Debian package names don't include underscores
pullRequest.body:
Debian prohibits the use of underscores in package names. This PR modifies the Debian package name to use bundle name, rather than the app name, as the basis for the installed package.

It also ensures that the bundle ID and name are normalised to lower case (which should be required regardless, as domain names aren't case sensitive).

This means an app with formal name `Under World`, app name `Under_world`, and bundle of `Org.Example` will now have a bundle name of `under-world`, producing `under-world_0.0.1.deb`, started with `/usr/bin/under_world`, with a bundle ID of `org.example.under-world`.

Previously, this same configuration would not have been able to produced a Debian package; but it would have had a bundle ID of `Org.Example.Under-world` on other platforms. This case normalisation is strictly backwards incompatible, but would likely have been an error on many platforms anyway.

Fixes #2699.

pullRequest.title: Preserve extras when building local Linux requirements.
pullRequest.body:
As Linux installs need to work with Docker, any reference to a local package is converted into a wheel, and the install requirement converted into a reference to a local wheel install (relative to the bundle).

However, if a package specified as a local source package references extras (e.g., `../path/to/code[extra]`), this fails, because the `[extra]` part isn't part of the file name, so the project can't be found.

This PR modifies the lookup strategy to split off the extras, and perform wheel/path lookups based on the "base" part of the name. The install requirements are then re-written on the fly, *appending* the extra definition, and passed to the underlying install tooling.

This also modifies the build to use wheels for local builds, since all Python installs will be PEP517 builds now anyway.

* Closes #2700

pullRequest.title: Pin binaryornot dependency.
pullRequest.body:
BinaryOrNot v0.5.0 introduced binaryornot/binaryornot#642, preventing correct detection of PNG content (#642). 

v0.6.0 was released a couple of days later, fixing #642; but introduced binaryornot/binaryornot#649; this raises warnings because of ambiguous file handling, and Briefcase's elevation of warnings to errors causes catastrophic failures when running under dev mode.

This adds a pin to BinaryOrNot until we can confirm these errors are resolved.

Fixes #2712.

pullRequest.title: Check for Python x86 Interpreter on Apple Silicon Mac
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->

This PR fixes #2736. 

The problem this PR solves:
- While following the [beeware tutorial](https://tutorial.beeware.org/tutorial/tutorial-5/iOS/), I was unable to launch the app from the iOS simulator from the briefcase command `briefcase run iOS`.
- Upon further research, it was determined that while porting my x86 mac to my current Apple Silicon mac, I ended up with a Python interpreter environment that claimed to be running on both x86 and Apple Silicon. 
- This caused the briefcase command line tools to call the wrong scripts (the one for x86), leading to the failure of the app launch from the iOS simulator.
- This PR solves the underlying problem in that now the briefcase command line tools will raise an error if it is trying to call the Python x86 interpreter while on an Apple Silicon machine.

Changes implemented offline (not in briefcase) and testing:
- Uninstalled all Python interpreters on my machine and reinstalled Python 3.13 from Homebrew.
- Re-created the beeware tutorial project with the newly installed Python 3.13 in the venv. 
Ran the briefcase command `briefcase run iOS` and saw the the app launch in the iOS simulator and successfully interacted with the app in the simulator.

Changes implemented in briefcase: 
- `changes/2736.bugfix.md` file: Change document
- `src/briefcase/platforms/macOS/__init__.py` file: Added a `verify_tools()` function in the `macOSMixin` class to check for an x86 Python interpreter running on Apple Silicon hardware
- `tests/platforms/macOS/test_macOSMixin_verify_tools.py` file: Add a unit test to test the `macOSMixin.verify_tools()` function.

pullRequest.title: Use `-any` tag suffix as an indicator of pure status.
pullRequest.body:
Use the existence of a `-any` tag as evidence of a wheel being "pure", rather than the `Root-Is-Purelib` metadata tag.

`Root-Is-Purelib` isn't set consistency (or easily) by some build tools, and it's technically possible for a `Root-Is-Purelib` package to contain platform binaries in the `.data` folder. The existence of a `*-*-any` tag is a more reliable indicator.

Fixes #2753.

pullRequest.title: Allow MSI image decorations to be customised
pullRequest.body:
MSI installers have two customizable images:

* The image shown as the background of the first page of the installer
* The banner image used on the top of every subsequent page of the installer.

This PR allows those two images to be customised. If a path isn't provided as part of the template context, the default images are used.

Requires template modifications:
* beeware/briefcase-windows-app-template#90
* beeware/briefcase-windows-VisualStudio-template#91

Fixes #2782.

For testing purposes: here are some sample images that can be used:

[installer-banner.bmp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27040325/installer-banner.bmp)
[installer-dialog.bmp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27040328/installer-dialog.bmp)

configured with:
```
installer_banner = "./installer-banner.bmp"
installer_background = "./installer-dialog.bmp"
```

## PR Checklist:
<!--- Go over all the following points, and put an `x` in all the boxes that apply. -->
- [x] I will abide by the BeeWare Code of Conduct
- [x] I have read and have followed the **CONTRIBUTING.md** file
- [ ] This PR was generated or assisted using an AI tool

pullRequest.title: Add documentation for .NET installer configuration options.
pullRequest.body:
Adds an optional check for .NET runtimes to MSI installers.

The bulk of the changes in Briefcase itself are documentation; the only functional change is including the `netfx` plugin when invoking `wix.exe`. The functional changes are all in the templates:

* Refs beeware/briefcase-windows-app-template#88
* Refs beeware/briefcase-windows-VisualStudio-template#90

Fixes #2804.
